title: Concert Operate for Batch Processing
text: MD

title: Concert Operate for Batch Processing
text: Description: “Concert Operate for Batch Processing”

title: Concert Operate for Batch Processing
text:
The Why
Operations teams managing large-scale batch workloads face a common and costly problem: a single job failure produces a cascade of duplicate alerts and tickets, flooding incident queues with noise and slowing response to genuine issues. Without a correlation and deduplication layer, L1 operators spend significant time triaging tickets that represent the same underlying event.
IBM Concert Operate addresses this by sitting between the event source and the ticketing system, correlating, deduplicating, and suppressing noise before it becomes work.

Problem Details
Batch job failures in enterprise environments are rarely isolated. A single failed job triggers retries, dependency failures, and monitoring alerts, each potentially generating its own incident ticket. At scale, this creates three compounding problems:

Noise volume: operators receive many tickets for what is functionally one incident, making it difficult to identify what actually requires human intervention.
Cascade failures: when one job fails and its downstream dependencies follow, each dependency generates independent alerts. Without cross-event correlation, the root cause is buried under consequential noise.
Retry churn: automated retry logic can generate repeated alert cycles before a job ultimately succeeds or fails definitively. Each retry cycle adds tickets with no incremental signal value.

The result is slower mean time to resolution (MTTR), higher operator fatigue, and reduced confidence in the alert signal.


Additional Context
The integration path for batch event ingestion depends on the existing operations toolchain. In environments using a batch scheduler with an event forwarding agent (such as a Tivoli postemsg-style mechanism), the near-term approach is to retarget that agent to deliver events to Concert Operate rather than the legacy event aggregator. This requires no changes to the batch scheduler itself. Only the outbound destination configuration changes.
A longer-term ingestion path using IBM Z Common Data Provider (CDP) for syslog-based detection is available in environments with Concert for Z licensing and z/OS infrastructure.

title: Solution Overview
text:
Concert Operate for Batch Processing: The What
IBM Concert Operate is an AIOps platform deployed on OpenShift Container Platform (OCP) that provides event correlation, noise reduction, and runbook automation. In this solution, it is positioned as the intelligence layer between a batch job event source and an ITSM ticketing system.
The core capability applied here is temporal and scope-based event correlation: Concert Operate groups related events (those that occur within a defined time window and match a defined scope: job name, job group, or dependency chain) into a single correlated incident. Only one ticket is raised for the correlated group, suppressing the duplicate and cascential noise that would otherwise flood the queue.
Key capabilities used in this solution:

Temporal correlation (“x events in y minutes”): groups repeated alerts from the same source into a single incident once a persistence threshold is crossed
Scope-based grouping: correlates events across a job dependency chain into one incident
Deduplication: suppresses duplicate tickets for the same underlying failure
Runbook automation: triggers a defined automated response (e.g. job retry) when a correlation policy fires

Why Concert Operate over alternatives:
The solution is deployed self-hosted on OCP, which fits environments with strict data residency or air-gap requirements. It integrates with existing event forwarding mechanisms without requiring changes to the batch scheduler or monitoring toolchain. Only the outbound event destination is retargeted.

title: Validate the OCP Cluster
text:
Run these checks before deploying Concert Operate. They catch environment blockers early and avoid a failed or incomplete installation. At the end of this step you will have a confirmed go / no-go for the cluster.
These checks apply to a Concert Operate v5.1.0 production base deployment on a self-hosted OpenShift Container Platform (OCP) cluster. Hardware requirements are sourced from the IBM Concert Operate v5.1.0 documentation (Table 4: Concert Operate + OpenShift, new cluster, production base, without Hybrid storage).

title: Validate the OCP Cluster
text:
Prerequisites

oc CLI installed and authenticated (oc login confirmed)
aiopsctl v5.1.0 installed (required for storage benchmark and precheck; see Install section below)
Cluster-admin or equivalent read access to inspect nodes, storage, and network resources
Namespace creation rights (or a pre-created target namespace)
Contact established with the platform / storage team for items that cannot be confirmed from the CLI alone (StorageClass filesystem type, NetworkPolicy defaults)


Install aiopsctl
Download the binary for your platform and put it on your PATH. Use the version-pinned URL to ensure you have v5.1.0.
# Replace the filename with your platform:
# aiopsctl-linux_amd64.tar.gz  |  aiopsctl-linux_arm64.tar.gz
# aiopsctl-darwin_amd64.tar.gz |  aiopsctl-darwin_arm64.tar.gz
# Windows: run from a WSL terminal using the linux_amd64 binary

AIOPSCTL_TAR=aiopsctl-linux_amd64.tar.gz
curl -LO "https://github.com/IBM/aiopsctl/releases/download/v5.1.0/${AIOPSCTL_TAR}"
tar xf "${AIOPSCTL_TAR}"
mv aiopsctl /usr/local/bin/aiopsctl
aiopsctl version   # expected: v5.1.0

title: Validate the OCP Cluster
text:
1. OCP Version and Architecture
Concert Operate v5.1.0 supports OCP v4.16 through v4.21 on x86_64 only. ARM and ppc64le architectures are not supported.
oc version
Expected output (example):
Client Version: 4.18.x
Server Version: 4.18.x
Kubernetes Version: v1.31.x
For architecture, check the KERNEL-VERSION column of oc get nodes -o wide. On x86_64 nodes the kernel version ends in .x86_64:
oc get nodes -o wide
Expected KERNEL-VERSION value (example):
5.14.0-427.130.1.el9_4.x86_64
All nodes must show .x86_64 in their kernel version. Any other suffix (.aarch64, .s390x) means the architecture requirement is not met.








Check
Command
What to look for




OCP version in range
oc version
Server Version: 4.16.x – 4.21.x


Node architecture
oc get nodes -o wide
All nodes: KERNEL-VERSION ends in .x86_64

title: Validate the OCP Cluster
text:
2. Compute: Worker Nodes
The production base profile requires a minimum of 6 worker nodes. Per-node minimums are 16 vCPU and 20 GB RAM. The cluster as a whole must satisfy the aggregate totals: 158 vCPU and 384 GB RAM across all workers. If nodes are provisioned below the per-node minimums, additional nodes are required to reach those totals.
Count worker nodes:
oc get nodes --selector='node-role.kubernetes.io/worker'
Capacity and Allocatable for all workers at once:
oc describe node is verbose. Use jsonpath to pull just the numbers you need across all worker nodes in one pass:
for node in $(oc get nodes --selector='node-role.kubernetes.io/worker' -o name); do
  echo "--- ${node} ---"
  oc get ${node} -o jsonpath=\
'CPU capacity: {.status.capacity.cpu}{"\n"}'\
'CPU allocatable: {.status.allocatable.cpu}{"\n"}'\
'Memory capacity (GiB): {.status.capacity.memory}{"\n"}'\
'Memory allocatable (GiB): {.status.allocatable.memory}{"\n"}'\
'Ephemeral storage capacity (GiB): {.status.capacity.ephemeral-storage}{"\n"}'\
'Ephemeral storage allocatable (GiB): {.status.allocatable.ephemeral-storage}{"\n"}'
done
Memory and storage values are returned in Ki (kibibytes). Divide by 1,048,576 to convert to GiB, or use oc adm top nodes for a live utilization view in human-readable units:
oc adm top nodes --selector='node-role.kubernetes.io/worker'
Aggregate capacity across all nodes (one line per node):
oc get nodes -o=custom-columns=\
'NAME:.metadata.name,'\
'CPU:.status.capacity.cpu,'\
'MEMORY_Ki:.status.capacity.memory,'\
'STORAGE_Ki:.status.capacity.ephemeral-storage'
Raw Ki values are expected here. For a node showing 131856920Ki memory, that is approximately 125 GiB (131,856,920 / 1,048,576). Multiply the per-node GiB by the worker count to confirm the cluster total meets 384 GB.
Allocatable is capacity minus OS and kubelet overhead. It is the figure that matters for scheduling.



Check
Per-node minimum
Aggregate target




Worker node count
n/a
≥ 6 nodes


vCPU per worker
≥ 16 vCPU
158 vCPU total


RAM per worker
≥ 20 GB
384 GB total


Ephemeral storage per worker
≥ 120 GB
n/a


Control plane nodes
n/a
3 nodes

title: Validate the OCP Cluster
text:
3. Storage: StorageClass
Storage class configuration cannot be changed after installation. Get this right before proceeding.

3a. Identify the StorageClass to use
Start by listing all available StorageClasses:
oc get sc
Example output:
NAME                          PROVISIONER                    RECLAIMPOLICY   VOLUMEBINDINGMODE      ALLOWVOLUMEEXPANSION
ocs-storagecluster-ceph-rbd   openshift-storage.rbd.csi...   Delete          Immediate              true
ocs-storagecluster-cephfs     openshift-storage.cephfs...    Delete          Immediate              true
The StorageClass for Concert Operate must be a block (RWO) class backed by a CSI driver. Look for classes provisioned by a block CSI driver (rbd, nutanix, portworx, gp3, etc.). CephFS and NFS classes provision filesystem (RWX) volumes; those are not the right choice for the primary Concert Operate storage class.
If multiple block classes exist, confirm which one is appropriate with your platform team. Note the name; you will use it in the installation CR and in the checks below. Replace <storage-class-name> with that name throughout this section.
The oc get sc output already shows ALLOWVOLUMEEXPANSION and VOLUMEBINDINGMODE for all classes at a glance. Use oc get sc <storage-class-name> -o yaml for full detail on a specific class.


3b. Verify filesystem type is XFS
MinIO requires XFS. An ext4 filesystem will exhaust inodes even when PVC space is available, leaving the filesystem read-only with no recovery path.
oc get sc <storage-class-name> -o yaml | grep -E 'fsType|csi.storage.k8s.io/fstype'
Look for fsType: xfs or csi.storage.k8s.io/fstype: xfs in the parameters block. If the field is missing or set to ext4, the StorageClass must be reconfigured or a new XFS-configured class must be requested from the storage team before installation.

Note: Some CSI drivers (e.g. Nutanix CSI) default to ext4. Confirm explicitly with the platform team. Do not assume the default is XFS.



3c. Verify allowVolumeExpansion: true
Required for runtime PVC resizing without downtime:
oc get sc <storage-class-name> -o yaml | grep allowVolumeExpansion
Must return allowVolumeExpansion: true. If it returns false or is absent, ask the platform team to enable it on the StorageClass before installation.


3d. Check volumeBindingMode
oc get sc <storage-class-name> -o yaml | grep volumeBindingMode
Either Immediate or WaitForFirstConsumer is acceptable. Confirm it is compatible with your cluster topology (single-zone vs. multi-zone).


3e. PVC smoke test
Create a small test PVC to confirm the StorageClass provisions and binds successfully. Replace <storage-class-name> with the confirmed class name.
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: concert-storage-smoke-test
  namespace: default
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  storageClassName: <storage-class-name>
  volumeMode: Filesystem
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 10Gi
oc apply -f smoke-test-pvc.yaml
oc get pvc concert-storage-smoke-test   # expect: Bound within ~60 seconds
oc delete pvc concert-storage-smoke-test
If the PVC stays in Pending, check events for provisioning errors:
oc describe pvc concert-storage-smoke-test

title: Validate the OCP Cluster
text:
4. Storage: Capacity and Performance

4a. Total PVC capacity
The production profile requires 2,627 Gi of usable PVC capacity. Check existing PV availability and confirm no pre-existing large claims will crowd out the installation:
oc get pv
oc get pvc -A   # check all namespaces for existing large claims


4b. IOPS: benchmark with aiopsctl
Run the built-in storage benchmark against your block StorageClass. The namespace must already exist; create it first if needed:
oc new-project <TARGET_NAMESPACE>
Then run the benchmark. It takes approximately 8 minutes and will prompt for confirmation before adding load to the cluster.
aiopsctl benchmark storage \
  --namespace=<TARGET_NAMESPACE> \
  --storage-class=<storage-class-name>
If storage performance varies significantly by node (e.g. nodes have different underlying disk tiers), target a specific node:
aiopsctl benchmark storage \
  --namespace=<TARGET_NAMESPACE> \
  --storage-class=<storage-class-name> \
  --node=<node-name>
Example output:
Benchmark Results:
Measurement                 Read                           Write
Sequential IOPS             10831.66      ✔ (> 5000 )      6665.42       ✔ (> 5000 )
Sequential Bandwidth        40.87 Mi/sec  ✔ (> 20 Mi/sec)  26.08 Mi/sec  ✔ (> 20 Mi/sec)
Average-Sequential Latency  626.56 usec   ✖ (< 500 usec)   1133.56 usec  ✖ (< 1000 usec)
Mixed-Sequential IOPS       2260.35                        6778.59
Minimum passing thresholds:



Metric
Read
Write




Minimum sequential IOPS
5,000
5,000


Minimum sequential bandwidth
20 MiB/s
20 MiB/s


Maximum average sequential latency
500 µsec
1,000 µsec



IOPS and bandwidth failures are hard blockers. Latency failures are a warning. Network-attached storage often exceeds the latency thresholds under normal conditions while still being suitable for Concert Operate in practice. If latency fails, note it and raise it with the storage team, but do not halt validation solely on latency. IOPS and bandwidth must pass.
These thresholds do not apply to native public cloud storage (Amazon EBS, IBM Cloud Block Storage).

title: Validate the OCP Cluster
text:
5. Network

5a. Cluster egress to the private container registry
Confirm the cluster can reach your private registry from within the cluster nodes, not just from a workstation. Use a debug pod on a worker node:
# Option 1: curl from the node host
oc debug node/<worker-node-name> -- chroot /host \
  curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" https://<REGISTRY_HOST>

# Expected: 200 or 401 (auth challenge means network is reachable)

# Option 2: pull a known image via crictl
oc debug node/<worker-node-name> -- chroot /host \
  crictl pull <REGISTRY_HOST>/cpopen/<any-image>
Replace <REGISTRY_HOST> with your private registry hostname (Artifactory or equivalent).
The following IBM image source paths must be reachable through the registry proxy:

icr.io/cpopen
icr.io/cpopen/cpfs
cp.icr.io/cp
cp.icr.io/cp/cp4waiops



5b. NetworkPolicy defaults
Check whether the cluster applies default-deny NetworkPolicies to new namespaces:
oc get networkpolicy -A
If default-deny policies are in place, add explicit allow rules for the Concert Operate namespace before installation. Check also for any proxy configuration that may need noProxy entries for the registry hostname or the Concert Operate namespace:
oc get proxy cluster -o yaml
Confirm with the platform team before proceeding if either applies.

title: Validate the OCP Cluster
text:
6. Namespace and RBAC

6a. Create the target namespace
If you have not already created the namespace for the storage benchmark above, create it now:
oc new-project <TARGET_NAMESPACE>
If the namespace was pre-created by the platform team, verify you can access it:
oc get namespace <TARGET_NAMESPACE>
oc project <TARGET_NAMESPACE>


6b. Confirm your user has sufficient rights
Concert Operate installation requires cluster-admin or an equivalent role that can create CRDs and cluster-scoped resources. Check your current user:
oc auth can-i '*' '*'
If this returns yes, you have cluster-admin. If it returns no, check what cluster roles are bound to your user:
oc get clusterrolebindings -o wide | grep $(oc whoami)
The install role must cover CRD creation, OperatorGroup and Subscription creation, and namespace-scoped resource management. If the role is unclear, confirm with the platform team before starting the installation.

title: Validate the OCP Cluster
text:
7. Run the aiopsctl Precheck
Run the built-in precheck to validate OCP version, hardware, and storage in one pass. At this stage, before cert-manager is installed, one check is expected to fail.
aiopsctl server precheck -n <TARGET_NAMESPACE>
Expected results on a fresh cluster:








Check
Expected result
Notes




Meets OCP Version Requirement
Passed
Must pass


Meets Hardware Requirements
Passed
Must pass


No Storage Issues
Passed
Must pass


Certificates Valid
Passed
Must pass


Cert Mgr Operator Exists
Failed
Expected at this stage; cert-manager is installed during the install step



If any check other than Cert Mgr Operator Exists fails, resolve it before proceeding to installation. A Cert Mgr Operator Exists failure here is normal and does not block you.

title: Validate the OCP Cluster
section: What to do with your results
text:
What to do with your results
At this point you have run all pre-installation checks. Two types of findings are possible.
Hard blockers (resolve before installing):

OCP version outside v4.16–v4.21
Non-x86_64 architecture on any node
Worker node count or aggregate vCPU / RAM below minimums
StorageClass not XFS, allowVolumeExpansion: false, or PVC smoke test fails
Storage benchmark IOPS or bandwidth below threshold
Registry unreachable from cluster nodes
Current user lacks sufficient RBAC to install
Any precheck result other than Cert Mgr Operator Exists fails

Expected at this stage (not blockers):

Cert Mgr Operator Exists fails in the precheck (cert-manager gets installed during the install step)
Latency benchmark warnings from network-attached storage (note them; raise with storage team if above 2x the threshold)

When all hard blockers are resolved, proceed to Install Concert Operate.

title: Install Concert Operate
text:
This page covers the deployment of Concert Operate onto an OpenShift Container Platform (OCP) cluster. At the end of this step, Concert Operate is running and accessible in the target namespace.
These instructions are based on the official IBM documentation: Installing IBM Concert Operate in an air-gapped environment using a bastion host

title: Install Concert Operate
text:
Prerequisites

OCP cluster provisioned and accessible (oc login confirmed)
Cluster-admin privileges
Storage class available with allowVolumeExpansion: true (see Environment)
Concert Operate images available in your private container registry. The following source paths must be accessible:

icr.io/cpopen
cp.icr.io/cp

oc CLI installed
ibm-pak plugin v1.23.0 installed
aiopsctl v5.1.0 installed

Download and install ibm-pak v1.23.0:
curl -LO https://github.com/IBM/ibm-pak/releases/download/v1.23.0/oc-ibm_pak-linux-amd64.tar.gz
tar -xf oc-ibm_pak-linux-amd64.tar.gz
mv oc-ibm_pak-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/oc-ibm_pak
oc ibm-pak --version   # Expected: v1.23.0
Download and install aiopsctl v5.1.0:
curl -LO "https://github.com/IBM/aiopsctl/releases/download/v5.1.0/aiopsctl-linux_amd64.tar.gz"
tar xf "aiopsctl-linux_amd64.tar.gz"
mv aiopsctl /usr/local/bin/aiopsctl
aiopsctl --version
Use the appropriate archive for your platform: aiopsctl-linux_amd64.tar.gz, aiopsctl-linux_s390x.tar.gz, aiopsctl-linux_arm64.tar.gz, aiopsctl-darwin_amd64.tar.gz, or aiopsctl-darwin_arm64.tar.gz. For Windows, run from a WSL terminal.

title: Install Concert Operate
text:
Procedure

Step 1: Set environment variables
Create and source an environment variable script. Replace the storage class names and registry values with those confirmed for your environment.
cat << 'EOF' > ~/waiops_var_concert-operate.sh
export CP4AIOPS_NAME=ibm-cp-aiops
export CP4AIOPS_SIZE=large             # production base deployment
export SECURE_TUNNEL=false
export PROJECT_CP4AIOPS=concert-operate
export ACCEPT_LICENSE=true
export CATALOG_SRC_CP4AIOPS=ibm-aiops-catalog
export LOG_ANOMALY=false               # base deployment; set true for extended

# Storage: confirm storage class names with your storage team
export STG_CLASS=<RWO-storage-class-name>
export STG_CLASS_BLOCK=<RWO-storage-class-name>

# Hybrid storage: leave empty if not using hybrid storage
export IR_CORE_POSTGRES_LOCAL_STORAGE_CLASS=''
export TOPOLOGY_POSTGRES_LOCAL_STORAGE_CLASS=''
export KAFKA_LOCAL_STORAGE_CLASS=''

# Customer details
export CUSTOMER_NAME=<your company name>
export CUSTOMER_ICN=<your IBM Customer Number>
export CUSTOMER_ENVIRONMENT=poc

export INSTALL_MODE_NAMESPACE=${PROJECT_CP4AIOPS}
export TOPOLOGY_TERMINOLOGY=application
EOF

. ~/waiops_var_concert-operate.sh
echo "PROJECT: $PROJECT_CP4AIOPS | SIZE: $CP4AIOPS_SIZE | STG: $STG_CLASS"
For Red Hat OpenShift Data Foundation (ODF), the storage class values are:
export STG_CLASS=ocs-storagecluster-ceph-rbd
export STG_CLASS_BLOCK=ocs-storagecluster-ceph-rbd
For Nutanix CSI, confirm the storage class name with your platform team using oc get sc, then set both variables to the confirmed class name.


Step 2: Update the global cluster pull secret (if needed)
Your OCP cluster needs credentials to pull images from the registry where Concert Operate images are stored. If your cluster already has a pull secret configured for your private registry (whether that is an Artifactory instance, a mirrored registry, or another private repository), confirm with your platform team and skip this step.
If the credentials are not yet configured, add them now. For pulling directly from the IBM Entitled Registry, get your IBM Entitlement Key from MyIBM Container Software Library.
export ENTITLED_REGISTRY_PASSWORD=<your-ibm-entitlement-key>

# Pull current global pull secret
oc get secret/pull-secret -n openshift-config \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.\.dockerconfigjson}' | base64 -d > /tmp/pull-secret.json

# Add registry credentials
oc registry login \
  --registry="cp.icr.io" \
  --auth-basic="cp:${ENTITLED_REGISTRY_PASSWORD}" \
  --to=/tmp/pull-secret.json

# Apply updated pull secret
oc set data secret/pull-secret -n openshift-config \
  --from-file=.dockerconfigjson=/tmp/pull-secret.json

unset ENTITLED_REGISTRY_PASSWORD
Wait for all nodes to pick up the updated pull secret before proceeding:
oc get MachineConfigPool -w
All MachineConfigPools must show UPDATED=True and UPDATING=False before continuing.


Step 3: Download the CASE and generate IDMS manifests (if needed)
If Concert Operate images have already been mirrored to your private registry, skip this step and go directly to Step 4. The CASE download is only needed if you need to generate the IDMS from scratch or perform the image mirror.
export CASE_NAME=ibm-cp-waiops
export CASE_VERSION=2.0.0
export TARGET_REGISTRY=<your-artifactory-registry-hostname>

oc ibm-pak get ${CASE_NAME} --version ${CASE_VERSION}
oc ibm-pak generate mirror-manifests ${CASE_NAME} ${TARGET_REGISTRY} --version ${CASE_VERSION}
TARGET_REGISTRY is the hostname of your private registry. The catalog source collision warning about ibm-cs-install-catalog, ibm-events-operator-catalog, and ibm-pg-operator-catalog can be safely ignored.

Note: ibm-pak v1.23.0 is required. Earlier versions fail signature validation on sub-CASEs signed with newer keys (ibm-cs-iam) and abort manifest generation.



Step 4: Apply the IDMS
The IDMS (ImageDigestMirrorSet) tells OCP to redirect image pulls from the IBM public registry to your private registry. This step is required even if images were pre-mirrored by your platform team.
If you ran Step 3, apply the generated file directly:
oc apply -f ~/.ibm-pak/data/mirror/${CASE_NAME}/${CASE_VERSION}/image-digest-mirror-set.yaml
If you skipped Step 3, create the IDMS manually. Replace <REGISTRY_HOST> with the hostname of your private registry (Artifactory, a mirrored registry, or equivalent):
apiVersion: config.openshift.io/v1
kind: ImageDigestMirrorSet
metadata:
  name: ibm-cp-waiops
spec:
  imageDigestMirrors:
  - mirrors:
    - <REGISTRY_HOST>/cp
    source: cp.icr.io/cp
  - mirrors:
    - <REGISTRY_HOST>/cpopen
    source: icr.io/cpopen
# Apply and verify
oc apply -f <your-idms-file.yaml>
oc get imagedigestmirrorset

# Wait for all nodes to pick up the config
oc get MachineConfigPool -w
All MachineConfigPools must return to UPDATED=True before continuing.


Step 5: Create the catalog sources
cat << EOF | oc apply -f -
apiVersion: operators.coreos.com/v1alpha1
kind: CatalogSource
metadata:
  name: ${CATALOG_SRC_CP4AIOPS}
  namespace: ${INSTALL_MODE_NAMESPACE}
spec:
  displayName: ${CATALOG_SRC_CP4AIOPS}
  publisher: IBM Content
  sourceType: grpc
  image: icr.io/cpopen/ibm-aiops-catalog@sha256:61e2b08e3731ecd2bf9b8349ca1d836e288f11847f0ec43b1711b1558d8218ce
  grpcPodConfig:
    securityContextConfig: restricted
---
apiVersion: operators.coreos.com/v1alpha1
kind: CatalogSource
metadata:
  name: ibm-cert-manager-catalog
  namespace: openshift-marketplace
spec:
  displayName: ibm-cert-manager
  publisher: IBM
  sourceType: grpc
  image: icr.io/cpopen/ibm-cert-manager-operator-catalog@sha256:cdfefe057e75b30c50b1cf9f8c2a71a07b498d09bb5b74c454d3a0a7c11aa989
EOF
ibm-aiops-catalog is created in the Concert Operate namespace (${INSTALL_MODE_NAMESPACE}), not in openshift-marketplace. ibm-cert-manager-catalog goes in openshift-marketplace.
Verify both catalog sources are running:
oc get catalogsource ibm-cert-manager-catalog -n openshift-marketplace
oc get catalogsource ${CATALOG_SRC_CP4AIOPS} -n ${INSTALL_MODE_NAMESPACE}


Step 6: Create the namespace and run precheck
oc create namespace ${PROJECT_CP4AIOPS}

# Annotate for amd64 architecture
oc annotate namespace "${PROJECT_CP4AIOPS}" \
  openshift.io/node-selector="kubernetes.io/arch=amd64"

# Verify
oc get namespace ${PROJECT_CP4AIOPS}
Run the precheck to validate the cluster is ready:
aiopsctl server precheck -n ${PROJECT_CP4AIOPS}
All checks must pass before proceeding. Expected results:



Check
Expected result




Meets OCP Version Requirement
Passed


Cert Mgr Operator Exists
Passed


Meets Hardware Requirements
Passed


No Storage Issues
Passed


Certificates Valid
Passed




Note: If cert-manager is not yet installed, the Cert Mgr Operator Exists check will fail at this point. Proceed to Step 7 to install it, then re-run the precheck to confirm.



Step 7: Install Cert Manager
Skip this step if a certificate manager is already installed on the cluster.
cat << EOF | oc apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: ibm-cert-manager
---
apiVersion: operators.coreos.com/v1
kind: OperatorGroup
metadata:
  name: ibm-cert-manager-operator-group
  namespace: ibm-cert-manager
---
apiVersion: operators.coreos.com/v1alpha1
kind: Subscription
metadata:
  name: ibm-cert-manager-operator
  namespace: ibm-cert-manager
spec:
  channel: v4.2
  installPlanApproval: Automatic
  name: ibm-cert-manager-operator
  source: ibm-cert-manager-catalog
  sourceNamespace: openshift-marketplace
EOF
Wait for all 4 pods to reach Running before continuing:
oc -n ibm-cert-manager get pods -w
Expected output:
NAME                                        READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
cert-manager-cainjector-xxx                 1/1     Running   0          ...
cert-manager-controller-xxx                 1/1     Running   0          ...
cert-manager-webhook-xxx                    1/1     Running   0          ...
ibm-cert-manager-operator-xxx               1/1     Running   0          ...


Step 8: Install the Concert Operate operator
cat << EOF | oc apply -f -
apiVersion: operators.coreos.com/v1
kind: OperatorGroup
metadata:
  name: concert-operate-operator-group
  namespace: ${PROJECT_CP4AIOPS}
spec:
  targetNamespaces:
    - "${PROJECT_CP4AIOPS}"
---
apiVersion: operators.coreos.com/v1alpha1
kind: Subscription
metadata:
  name: ibm-aiops-orchestrator
  namespace: ${INSTALL_MODE_NAMESPACE}
spec:
  channel: v5.1
  installPlanApproval: Automatic
  name: ibm-aiops-orchestrator
  source: ${CATALOG_SRC_CP4AIOPS}
  sourceNamespace: ${INSTALL_MODE_NAMESPACE}
EOF
installPlanApproval must remain Automatic. Manual approval is not supported and can result in a failed installation.
Wait for the operator CSV to reach Succeeded:
oc get csv -n ${INSTALL_MODE_NAMESPACE} -w | grep ibm-aiops-orchestrator
Expected output:
ibm-aiops-orchestrator.v5.1.0   IBM Concert Operate   5.1.0   Succeeded


Step 9: Create the Installation CR
cat << EOF | oc apply -f -
apiVersion: orchestrator.aiops.ibm.com/v1alpha1
kind: Installation
metadata:
  name: ${CP4AIOPS_NAME}
  namespace: ${PROJECT_CP4AIOPS}
spec:
  size: ${CP4AIOPS_SIZE}
  storage:
    aiops-ir-core-postgres:
      storageClass: ${IR_CORE_POSTGRES_LOCAL_STORAGE_CLASS}
    aiops-ir-core-postgres-wal:
      storageClass: ${IR_CORE_POSTGRES_LOCAL_STORAGE_CLASS}
    aiops-topology-postgres:
      storageClass: ${TOPOLOGY_POSTGRES_LOCAL_STORAGE_CLASS}
    aiops-topology-postgres-wal:
      storageClass: ${TOPOLOGY_POSTGRES_LOCAL_STORAGE_CLASS}
    data-iaf-system-kafka:
      storageClass: ${KAFKA_LOCAL_STORAGE_CLASS}
  storageClass: ${STG_CLASS}
  storageClassLargeBlock: ${STG_CLASS_BLOCK}
  imagePullSecret:
  topologyModel: ${TOPOLOGY_TERMINOLOGY}
  license:
    accept: ${ACCEPT_LICENSE}
  pakModules:
  - name: aiopsFoundation
    enabled: true
  - name: applicationManager
    enabled: true
  - name: aiManager
    enabled: true
  - name: connection
    enabled: ${SECURE_TUNNEL}
  - name: logAnomalyDetection
    enabled: ${LOG_ANOMALY}
EOF
aiopsFoundation, applicationManager, and aiManager must remain enabled: true.
Monitor installation progress. The PHASE starts as Updating and changes to Running when complete. This takes 60-90 minutes:
oc get installations.orchestrator.aiops.ibm.com -n ${PROJECT_CP4AIOPS} -w
If the installation stalls, check for components that are not ready:
oc get installation.orchestrator.aiops.ibm.com -o yaml \
  -n ${PROJECT_CP4AIOPS} | grep 'Not Ready'

# Get detail on a specific not-ready component (e.g. zenservice)
oc get <component> -o yaml -n ${PROJECT_CP4AIOPS}

title: Install Concert Operate
text:
Verification
Once the installation phase shows Running, retrieve the console URL and credentials:
# Console URL
oc get route -n ${PROJECT_CP4AIOPS} cpd -o jsonpath='{.spec.host}'

# Admin username
oc -n ${PROJECT_CP4AIOPS} get secret platform-auth-idp-credentials \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.admin_username}' | base64 -d && echo

# Admin password
oc -n ${PROJECT_CP4AIOPS} get secret platform-auth-idp-credentials \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.admin_password}' | base64 -d && echo
Open the console URL in a browser and log in with the admin credentials to confirm the Concert Operate UI is accessible.

title: Key Takeaways
text: Best Practices

title: Configure Concert Operate
text:
Configure Concert Operate: The How
This page covers the post-install configuration of Concert Operate: setting up event ingestion, defining correlation policies, and configuring the ITSM integration. At the end of this step, Concert Operate should be receiving events and applying correlation rules.

Prerequisites

Concert Operate installed and running (see Install Concert Operate)
Event source system accessible and able to reach the Concert Operate ingestion endpoint
ServiceNow Event Management target available



Procedure








Verification

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Troubleshooting
This page captures known issues and resolutions encountered during deployment and configuration. It is updated as issues are discovered and resolved.

title: Environment
text:
Target Environment
Concert Operate is deployed on a self-hosted OpenShift Container Platform (OCP) cluster. The cluster is dedicated to the Concert Operate workload and provisioned with the following general characteristics:

Platform: OpenShift Container Platform (OCP), version confirmed at deployment time
Deployment model: Dedicated OCP cluster; Concert Operate deployed into a dedicated namespace
Container registry: Internal Artifactory instance acting as a pull-through proxy for icr.io and cp.icr.io. IBM entitlement key is not required when using this proxy path.
Storage: Nutanix CSI storage provisioner; xfs filesystem; storage class confirmed at deployment time
Image availability: All required Concert Operate images available via the Artifactory proxy: icr.io/cpopen, icr.io/cpopen/cpfs, cp.icr.io/cp, cp.icr.io/cp/cp4waiops paths all validated



Event Ingestion Path
Events originate from the batch scheduler’s operations management layer. The near-term ingestion path uses an existing event forwarding agent (postemsg-style) on the source system. The agent is retargeted to deliver events to Concert Operate rather than the legacy event aggregator. No changes to the batch scheduler itself are required.
Connectivity: The event source system must have outbound network access to the Concert Operate ingestion endpoint on <CONCERT_OPERATE_HOSTNAME>:<PORT>. Firewall rules and network security requests should be scoped to this single destination.


Integration Target
Correlated incidents are forwarded to ServiceNow Event Management as the ITSM target. This aligns with the client environment’s transition from a legacy event aggregator to ServiceNow Event Management as the primary event consolidation layer.
